1:
"And Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married: for he had married an Ethiopian woman." The very brief verse notes Miriam and Aaron speaking against Moses over his wife. The grammatical voices note the occasion. Henry and Gill note that "Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married" — Miriam and Aaron, Moses's own brother and sister, spoke against him, taking occasion from the Ethiopian (Cushite) woman he had married; the spake against Moses (the murmuring against God's chosen servant); the because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married (the pretext, the marriage made the occasion); the figure (the sad sight of the nearest kin turning against God's servant, envy rising even in the family; the pretext of the marriage masking the deeper grievance of envy at Moses's place, vv.2; the murmuring against the man God had set over them; the warning that even the godly may fall into envy and detraction, speaking against the Lord's anointed, Psalm 105:15).

On "Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses," the witnesses note the sedition. Gill notes Moses's brother and sister speaking against him (the murmuring of the kin) — the sedition (the speaking against God's servant).

On "because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married: for he had married an Ethiopian woman," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the pretext: "because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married" — the marriage the occasion, the pretext for envy (Psalm 105:15); the figure (the deeper grievance masked); the providence (the murmuring, the claim of equal prophecy to follow, vv.2). The verse displays Miriam and Aaron speaking against Moses over his wife.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the claim of equal prophecy (v.2).
2:
"And they said, Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? hath he not spoken also by us? And the LORD heard it." The very brief verse notes the claim of equal prophecy and the LORD hearing it. The grammatical voices note the envy. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they said, Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? hath he not spoken also by us? And the LORD heard it" — the real grievance: Miriam and Aaron envied Moses's unique authority, claiming they too were prophets; and the LORD heard their words; the Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? (the challenge to Moses's unique standing); the hath he not spoken also by us? (the claim of equal prophecy, Miriam a prophetess, Exodus 15:20, and Aaron the priest); the And the LORD heard it (God hearing the murmuring, the secret grievance not hidden from him); the figure (the envy of God's servant's special place, the grasping at equal honor; the pride that will not bear another's pre-eminence; the truth that God hears the murmuring of the heart, "the LORD heard it," nothing hidden from him, Malachi 3:16; the danger of envy at the gifts and place God gives to others).

On "Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? hath he not spoken also by us?" the witnesses note the claim. Gill notes the claim of equal prophecy (the envy of Moses's place, Exodus 15:20) — the envy (the grasping at honor).

On "And the LORD heard it," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note God's hearing: "And the LORD heard it" — God hearing the murmuring (nothing hidden, Malachi 3:16); the figure (the secret grievance known to God); the providence (the LORD hearing, the vindication of Moses to follow, vv.4-8). The verse displays the claim of equal prophecy and the LORD hearing it.

The verse displays the meekness of Moses (v.3).
3:
"(Now the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth.)" The witnesses note the meekness of Moses above all men — the crux of the meekness that figures Christ. On "the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth," interpreters expound the testimony. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the meekness — "the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth": in the midst of the attack on him, the Spirit records that Moses was the meekest of all men, that his vindication came not from himself but from God; the witnesses note the significance — the surpassing meekness of Moses (not defending himself, not resenting the wrong, leaving his cause to God); the lesson — that the meekness which does not avenge itself but leaves all to God is the highest grace (Moses, so provoked by his own kin, made no reply, but God arose to vindicate him; the meek who do not return the injury but commit themselves to the righteous Judge); the figure — Moses the meek a figure of Christ, "I am meek and lowly in heart," Matthew 11:29, "who, when he was reviled, reviled not again... but committed himself to him that judgeth righteously," 1 Peter 2:23; the meekness that inherits the earth, "blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth," Matthew 5:5; the figure of the servant of God who endures wrong without retaliation, leaving his cause to God, supremely fulfilled in Christ the meek and lowly. The figure of meekness vindicated by God.

On "the man Moses was very meek," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the meekness. Keil and Ellicott note Moses very meek (not avenging himself, leaving his cause to God, 1 Peter 2:23) — the meekness (the highest grace).

On "above all the men which were upon the face of the earth," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the surpassing grace. Wesley and Gill note Moses the meekest of all (the surpassing meekness, the figure of Christ, Matthew 11:29) — the figure (the meek who inherit the earth, Matthew 5:5); the providence (the meekness recorded, God's vindication to follow). The verse displays the meekness of Moses above all men.

The verse displays the meekness crux. The meekness of Moses above all men displays the crux: the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth (Matthew 11:29; 1 Peter 2:23; Matthew 5:5) — the surpassing meekness that does not avenge itself but commits all to God, a figure of Christ the meek and lowly, the servant vindicated by God. The verse displays God calling the three to the tabernacle (v.4).
4:
"And the LORD spake suddenly unto Moses, and unto Aaron, and unto Miriam, Come out ye three unto the tabernacle of the congregation. And they three came out." The very brief verse notes the LORD suddenly calling the three to the tabernacle. The grammatical voices note the summons. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD spake suddenly unto Moses, and unto Aaron, and unto Miriam, Come out ye three unto the tabernacle of the congregation. The verse displays the LORD suddenly calling the three to the tabernacle.

5:
"And the LORD came down in the pillar of the cloud, and stood in the door of the tabernacle, and called Aaron and Miriam: and they both came forth." The very brief verse notes the LORD coming down in the cloud to judge. The grammatical voices note the descent. 6:
"And he said, Hear now my words: If there be a prophet among you, I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream." The very brief verse notes God's ordinary way of speaking to prophets by vision and dream. The grammatical voices note the manner. Calvin and Gill note that "Hear now my words: If there be a prophet among you, I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream" — God declared his ordinary way of revealing himself to prophets: by vision and dream, not the close communion he had with Moses; the If there be a prophet among you (the ordinary prophet); the I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream (the manner of revelation to the prophets, indirect, by vision and dream); the figure (the lesser, mediated revelation given to the prophets, distinguished from the direct speech with Moses; the gradation of revelation, the prophets receiving by vision and dream, "God... spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets," Hebrews 1:1; the contrast that exalts Moses's closeness, vv.7-8; the manner of prophetic revelation, the foreshadowing of the fuller revelation to come).

On "If there be a prophet among you, I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision," the witnesses note the vision. Gill notes the ordinary prophet receiving by vision (the mediated revelation, Hebrews 1:1) — the manner (the vision).

The verse displays God's ordinary way of speaking to prophets.

The verse displays Moses faithful in all God's house (v.7).
7:
"My servant Moses is not so, who is faithful in all mine house." The very brief verse notes Moses faithful in all God's house. The grammatical voices note the faithfulness. Clarke and Gill note that "my servant Moses is not so, who is faithful in all mine house" — Moses was not like the ordinary prophets, for he was faithful in all God's house, trusted with the whole; the My servant Moses is not so (Moses above the ordinary prophets); the who is faithful in all mine house (the trusted steward, faithful over the whole household of God); the figure (Moses the faithful servant over all God's house, the steward trusted with the whole; the New Testament use, where Moses's faithfulness is the figure that exalts Christ: "Moses verily was faithful in all his house, as a servant... but Christ as a son over his own house," Hebrews 3:2-6; Moses faithful as a servant, Christ faithful as the Son and Lord of the house; the figure of the faithful steward, and of Christ the faithful Son over the house of God; the honor God puts on his faithful servant).

The verse displays Moses faithful in all God's house.

The verse displays God speaking to Moses mouth to mouth (v.8).
8:
"With him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the LORD shall he behold: wherefore then were ye not afraid to speak against my servant Moses?" The witnesses note God speaking with Moses mouth to mouth, and the rebuke of the offenders — the crux of Moses's unique nearness to God. On "with him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the LORD shall he behold," interpreters expound the vindication. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the close communion — "with him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the LORD shall he behold": God spoke with Moses directly, mouth to mouth, clearly and not in riddles, and Moses beheld the form of the LORD; the witnesses note the significance — the unique nearness of Moses to God (vv.6); the lesson — that God set Moses in a place of nearness above all, "the LORD spake unto Moses face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend," Exodus 33:11; Deuteronomy 34:10, "there arose not a prophet since in Israel like unto Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face"; the gravity of the rebuke — "wherefore then were ye not afraid to speak against my servant Moses?" (the offenders rebuked for daring to murmur against the man God so honored); the figure — Moses's nearness a figure of the yet greater nearness of Christ, who is in the bosom of the Father and has declared him, "no man hath seen God at any time; the only begotten Son... he hath declared him," John 1:18; the warning against speaking against the Lord's servants, "touch not mine anointed," Psalm 105:15;.

On "with him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the LORD shall he behold," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and witnesses note the communion. Ellicott and Gill note the direct, clear speech with Moses, beholding the form of the LORD (the unique nearness, Exodus 33:11; Deuteronomy 34:10) — the communion (the face-to-face nearness).

On "wherefore then were ye not afraid to speak against my servant Moses?" the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the rebuke. Wesley and Gill note the offenders rebuked for speaking against the honored servant (the holy fear due, Psalm 105:15) — the figure (the warning against detraction, John 1:18); the providence (the rebuke, the leprosy of Miriam to follow, vv.9-10). The verse displays God speaking with Moses mouth to mouth and rebuking the offenders.

The witnesses agree the substance: God spoke with Moses directly, mouth to mouth, and rebuked the offenders for speaking against him (Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of Moses's unique nearness to God: the mouth-to-mouth speech — the direct, clear communion of Moses with God, far above the prophets' vision and dream, who beheld the form of the LORD (Exodus 33:11; Deuteronomy 34:10), and the grave rebuke of those who dared speak against the man God so honored (Psalm 105:15), a figure of the yet greater nearness of Christ in the bosom of the Father (John 1:18). The verse displays the nearness crux. The verse displays the anger of the LORD and Miriam's leprosy (vv.9-10).
9:

10:
"And the cloud departed from off the tabernacle; and, behold, Miriam became leprous, white as snow: and Aaron looked upon Miriam, and, behold, she was leprous." The very brief verse notes Miriam stricken with leprosy white as snow. The grammatical voices note the judgment. Henry and Gill note that "the cloud departed from off the tabernacle; and, behold, Miriam became leprous, white as snow: and Aaron looked upon Miriam, and, behold, she was leprous" — as the cloud departed, Miriam was struck with leprosy, white as snow, and Aaron saw her; the the cloud departed from off the tabernacle (the presence withdrawn); the Miriam became leprous, white as snow (the judgment, the leprosy of the worst kind); the Aaron looked upon Miriam... she was leprous (the priest beholding the judgment); the figure (the leprosy the visible judgment on the sin, the murmuring punished with the defiling disease; Miriam, the leader in the sin, struck, while Aaron the priest spared (perhaps for his office), yet rebuked by the sight; the figure that sin defiles and is judged, the leprosy a token of the uncleanness of sin; the warning of the judgment on detraction and envy, the visible mark of God's displeasure).

11:
"And Aaron said unto Moses, Alas, my lord, I beseech thee, lay not the sin upon us, wherein we have done foolishly, and wherein we have sinned." The verse displays Aaron's confession and appeal to Moses.

12:
"Let her not be as one dead, of whom the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb." The very brief verse notes Aaron's pleading for Miriam's life. The grammatical voices note the plea. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Let her not be as one dead, of whom the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb" — Aaron pleaded that Miriam not be left like a stillborn child, half consumed, for the leprosy was wasting her; the Let her not be as one dead (the plea against the wasting death); the of whom the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb (the figure of the leprosy consuming the flesh as a stillbirth); the figure (the dreadful wasting of the leprosy likened to a corrupt stillbirth, the flesh consumed; the plea for mercy on the stricken, the appeal to spare her from the consuming disease; the figure of the corruption and death that sin works, the flesh consumed, a vivid picture of the wasting of the body under the judgment; the cry for life against the consuming death of the leprosy).

On "Let her not be as one dead," the witnesses note the plea. Gill notes the plea against the wasting death (the leprosy consuming) — the plea (against the death).

On "of whom the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb," the grammatical-historical JFB and grammatical-historical note the consuming: "the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb" — the leprosy consuming the flesh as a stillbirth (the wasting disease); the figure (the corruption sin works on the flesh); the providence (the plea for Miriam, Moses's prayer to follow, vv.13). The verse displays Aaron's pleading for Miriam's life.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the plea — "Let her not be as one dead, of whom the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb," the plea (Keil, Gill); "Let her not be as one dead," the plea (Gill); "of whom the flesh is half consumed when he cometh out of his mother's womb," the consuming (JFB, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the plea. The verse marks Miriam: not as dead. The verse displays the flesh: half consumed. The verse displays the leprosy: wasting. The verse displays the mercy: sought. The plea displays the appeal for mercy on the stricken. The verse displays the death: feared. The verse displays the flesh: consumed. The verse displays the cry: for life. Aaron's pleading for Miriam's life displays the cry for mercy: Let her not be as one dead, of whom the flesh is half consumed — the dreadful wasting of the leprosy likened to a corrupt stillbirth, the appeal to spare her from the consuming disease, the cry for life against the death of the leprosy. The verse displays Moses's prayer for Miriam's healing (v.13).
13:
"And Moses cried unto the LORD, saying, Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee." The witnesses note Moses's prayer for the healing of Miriam — the crux of the meek servant interceding for his enemies. On "Moses cried unto the LORD, saying, Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee," interpreters expound the intercession. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the prayer — "Moses cried unto the LORD, saying, Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee": Moses, the very one Miriam had wronged, cried to God to heal her, with a brief and earnest prayer; the witnesses note the significance — the meek servant interceding for the one who had wronged him (Moses, so far from resenting Miriam's sin, prayed at once for her healing); the lesson — the highest expression of the meekness recorded in vv.3 (the wronged praying for the wrongdoer, returning good for evil, the love that intercedes for its enemies); the brevity and earnestness of the prayer ("Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee" — the short, fervent cry of love); the figure — Moses a figure of Christ, who prayed for his enemies, "Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do," Luke 23:34; the love that "blesses them which persecute you," "pray for them which despitefully use you," Matthew 5:44; Romans 12:14; the intercession of the meek for those who wrong them, supremely fulfilled in Christ who intercedes for his people and prayed for his crucifiers; the figure of forgiving love that turns at once to prayer for the offender; the truth that the meek do not avenge but intercede. The figure of the wronged servant praying for the healing of the one who wronged him.

On "Moses cried unto the LORD," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and witnesses note the cry. Ellicott and Gill note Moses crying to God at once (the earnest, fervent prayer) — the cry (the prayer to God).

On "Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the intercession. Gill notes Moses praying for the healing of the one who wronged him (the meek interceding for his enemy, Luke 23:34; Matthew 5:44) — the figure (the forgiving love, Christ praying for his crucifiers); the providence (the prayer, God's answer to follow, vv.14). The verse displays Moses's prayer for the healing of Miriam.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses, the wronged, cried to God to heal Miriam (Calvin, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the meek servant interceding for his enemies: Moses's prayer — the wronged praying at once for the wrongdoer, the highest expression of his surpassing meekness (vv.3), returning good for evil and interceding for the one who sinned against him, a figure of Christ who prayed for his enemies and crucifiers (Luke 23:34; Matthew 5:44; Romans 12:14). The verse displays the intercession crux. The verse marks Moses: crying. The verse displays the prayer: heal her. The verse displays the wronged: interceding. The verse displays the Christ: praying for his enemies. The prayer displays the meek interceding for the wrongdoer. The verse displays the love: forgiving. The verse displays the cry: for healing. The verse displays the Christ: who forgives. Moses's prayer for the healing of Miriam displays the crux: Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee (Luke 23:34; Matthew 5:44; Romans 12:14) — the meek servant interceding at once for the one who wronged him, returning good for evil, a figure of Christ who prayed for his enemies and crucifiers. The verse displays God's answer, Miriam shut out seven days (vv.14-15).
14:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, If her father had but spit in her face, should she not be ashamed seven days? let her be shut out from the camp seven days, and after that let her be received in again." The very brief verse notes God's answer, Miriam shut out seven days. The grammatical voices note the chastening. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "If her father had but spit in her face, should she not be ashamed seven days? let her be shut out from the camp seven days, and after that let her be received in again" — God answered that Miriam, though healed, must bear the shame of her sin, shut out of the camp seven days, as a daughter shamed by a father's rebuke; the If her father had but spit in her face, should she not be ashamed seven days? (the figure of a father's open rebuke, the shame to be borne); the let her be shut out from the camp seven days (the exclusion, the bearing of the shame); the after that let her be received in again (the restoration after the chastening); the figure (the chastening that, though the sin is forgiven, requires the bearing of the shame and the discipline; the mercy that heals yet disciplines, the forgiven still bearing the chastisement; the figure that God's children, though pardoned, may be chastened for their sins, "whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth," Hebrews 12:6; the shame and exclusion borne, then the restoration; the discipline that humbles before it restores).

On "If her father had but spit in her face, should she not be ashamed seven days?" the witnesses note the shame. Gill notes the figure of the father's rebuke and the shame to be borne (the chastening) — the shame (the discipline).

On "let her be shut out from the camp seven days, and after that let her be received in again," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the exclusion and restoration: "let her be shut out from the camp seven days... after that let her be received in again" — the exclusion and then restoration (the chastening, then the receiving back, Hebrews 12:6); the figure (the forgiven still chastened, then restored); the providence (Miriam shut out, the people staying for her, vv.15). The verse displays God's answer, Miriam shut out seven days.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the chastening — "let her be shut out from the camp seven days, and after that let her be received in again," the chastening (Keil, Gill); "If her father had but spit in her face, should she not be ashamed seven days?" the shame (Gill); "let her be shut out from the camp seven days, and after that let her be received in again," the exclusion and restoration (Hebrews 12:6, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the chastening. The verse marks Miriam: shut out. The verse displays the shame: seven days. The verse displays the restoration: received again. The verse displays the discipline: of God. The chastening displays the forgiven still bearing the discipline. The verse displays the shame: borne. The verse displays the exclusion: seven days. The verse displays the restoration: after. God's answer, Miriam shut out seven days, displays the chastening discipline: let her be shut out from the camp seven days, and after that let her be received in again (Hebrews 12:6) — the mercy that heals yet disciplines, the forgiven bearing the shame and exclusion before the restoration, the chastening that humbles before it restores. The verse displays the people staying for Miriam (v.15).
15:
"And Miriam was shut out from the camp seven days: and the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again." The very brief verse notes Miriam shut out and the people staying for her. The grammatical voices note the waiting. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Miriam was shut out from the camp seven days: and the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again" — Miriam bore her seven days' exclusion, and the whole people waited, not journeying until she was restored; the Miriam was shut out from the camp seven days (the chastening borne); the the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again (the whole camp waiting for her restoration); the figure (the people staying for the one shut out, the patience of God's people with the chastened; the honor God put on Miriam even in her chastening, the host waiting for her; the figure of the body that waits for its members, none left behind, the weak and the chastened borne with; the truth that God's people are knit together, the whole staying for the one, "the members should have the same care one for another," 1 Corinthians 12:25; the patience that waits for the restoration of the fallen).

On "Miriam was shut out from the camp seven days," the witnesses note the exclusion. Gill notes Miriam bearing the seven days' exclusion (the chastening) — the exclusion (the chastening borne).

On "the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the waiting: "the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again" — the whole camp waiting for her restoration (the patience with the chastened, 1 Corinthians 12:25); the figure (the body waiting for its members); the providence (Miriam restored, the journey resumed, vv.16). The verse displays Miriam shut out and the people staying for her.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the waiting — "Miriam was shut out from the camp seven days: and the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again," the waiting (Keil, Gill); "Miriam was shut out from the camp seven days," the exclusion (Gill); "the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again," the waiting (1 Corinthians 12:25, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the waiting. The verse marks Miriam: shut out. The verse displays the people: waiting. The verse displays the camp: staying. The verse displays the patience: with the chastened. The waiting displays the people staying for the one shut out. The verse displays Miriam: chastened. The verse displays the host: waiting. The verse displays the restoration: awaited. Miriam shut out and the people staying for her display the patience with the chastened: the people journeyed not till Miriam was brought in again (1 Corinthians 12:25) — the whole camp waiting for her restoration, the body bearing with its members, none left behind, the patience that waits for the restoration of the fallen. The verse displays the journey to the wilderness of Paran (v.16).
16:
"And afterward the people removed from Hazeroth, and pitched in the wilderness of Paran." The very brief verse notes the journey to the wilderness of Paran, closing the chapter. The grammatical voices note the journey. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "afterward the people removed from Hazeroth, and pitched in the wilderness of Paran" — after Miriam's restoration, the people journeyed from Hazeroth and encamped in the wilderness of Paran; the afterward the people removed from Hazeroth (the journey resumed after the chastening); the and pitched in the wilderness of Paran (the next encampment, the place from which the spies would be sent, Numbers 13:3); the figure (the journey continued after the discipline, the people moving on; the pilgrimage resumed, the camp at Paran whence the spies would go; the truth that God's people, after the chastening, journey on under his guidance; the encampment at Paran the setting for the great trial of faith to come, the sending of the spies, vv.13).

On "afterward the people removed from Hazeroth," the witnesses note the journey. Gill notes the journey resumed after the chastening (the people moving on) — the journey (from Hazeroth).

On "and pitched in the wilderness of Paran," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the encampment: "pitched in the wilderness of Paran" — the camp at Paran (the place of the spies, Numbers 13:3); the figure (the pilgrimage resumed, the setting for the trial to come); the providence (the camp at Paran, the spies to be sent, ch. 13). The verse displays the journey to the wilderness of Paran.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the journey — "afterward the people removed from Hazeroth, and pitched in the wilderness of Paran," the journey (Keil, Gill); "afterward the people removed from Hazeroth," the journey (Gill); "and pitched in the wilderness of Paran," the encampment (Numbers 13:3, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the journey. The verse marks the people: removed. The verse displays Hazeroth: left. The verse displays Paran: the camp. The verse displays the pilgrimage: resumed. The journey displays the people moving on after the chastening. The verse displays the journey: continued. The verse displays the camp: at Paran. The verse displays the trial: to come. The journey to the wilderness of Paran displays the pilgrimage resumed: pitched in the wilderness of Paran (Numbers 13:3) — the journey continued after the discipline, the camp at Paran whence the spies would be sent, the setting for the great trial of faith to come. The verse closes the chapter, before the sending of the spies (ch.13).
